title: How to Talk to Your Child's Teacher When You Suspect a Learning Difference
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You have noticed something. Your child avoids reading aloud. They cry before school on spelling test days. They spend hours on homework that should take twenty minutes. The teacher has not raised any concerns, but your instinct tells you something is not right. You want to bring it up, but you do not know how. You do not want to seem like an overanxious parent. You do not want to criticize the teacher. You do not want to label your child unnecessarily. So you wait. The waiting is understandable, but it is not helpful. Early intervention for learning differences is more effective than waiting to see if the problem resolves on its own. Here is how to have this conversation respectfully and productively.

Why Parents Delay and Why They Should Not

Parents delay raising concerns for many reasons. Some worry about stigmatizing their child. Some have been told by well-meaning family members that the child will grow out of it. Some had difficult school experiences themselves and find interactions with teachers intimidating. Some assume that if there were a real problem, the teacher would have noticed and said something. These reasons are all understandable. They are also all worth overcoming.

Teachers are knowledgeable, but they are not diagnosticians. They observe your child in a group setting for several hours a day. They may notice that your child struggles with certain tasks, but they may attribute those struggles to attention, effort, or maturity rather than to an underlying learning difference. They also see a wide range of developmental variation and may not flag a child who is struggling but still within the broad range of normal. Your perspective as the parent, observing your child one-on-one over years, is different from and complementary to the teacher's perspective. You are not second-guessing the teacher. You are adding information they do not have.

Early intervention is effective. Learning differences identified in kindergarten or first grade are much easier to remediate than those identified in third or fourth grade, after years of frustration and avoidance have compounded the academic gap. The child who receives reading support at six may catch up to peers within a year. The child who does not receive support until nine has a much longer road. The earlier the conversation happens, the better the outcome.

How to Prepare for the Conversation

Before you request a meeting, document what you are seeing at home. Keep a log for two weeks. Note specific behaviors: avoids reading, complains of headaches during homework, reverses letters beyond what is typical for the age, struggles to follow multi-step directions, forgets instructions from one day to the next. Include frequency and context. The log transforms a vague concern into specific, discussable data. Instead of saying she has a hard time with reading, you can say she cried during reading homework four times in the past two weeks, and when I asked what was hard, she said the words move around on the page. The specificity gives the teacher something concrete to respond to.

Request a meeting rather than trying to have the conversation at drop-off or pickup. A brief hallway conversation does not provide enough time or privacy for a serious discussion. Email the teacher and say you would like to discuss some observations about your child's learning and would appreciate a time to talk. You do not need to provide details in the email. The meeting request signals that this is important without raising alarm.

Prepare questions rather than conclusions. Do not walk into the meeting and announce that you believe your child has dyslexia or ADHD. You may be right, but you may also be wrong, and the teacher is more likely to engage productively with questions than with amateur diagnoses. Ask: Have you noticed her struggling with letter sounds? Does she seem to lose focus more than other children her age? How does her reading compare to what you expect at this point in the year? The questions invite the teacher's observations and open a collaborative inquiry rather than a defensive debate.

During the Meeting

Start by acknowledging the teacher's expertise and the constraints of their role. I know you are with many children every day, and I appreciate how well you know my child. I wanted to share some things I am seeing at home and hear what you are seeing in the classroom. The opening establishes respect and positions the conversation as a partnership.

Present your observations without emotion. Stick to the facts you documented. Avoid phrases like I am really worried or this has been keeping me up at night, even if they are true. The teacher needs information, not emotion. An emotional presentation can make the teacher feel blamed or pressured, which reduces their ability to respond thoughtfully. Present the facts calmly and let the teacher respond.

Ask what interventions are available within the school. Most schools have a multi-tiered system of support that provides increasingly intensive interventions based on student need. The first tier is classroom-based support: small group instruction, modified assignments, or preferential seating. The second tier is targeted intervention: pull-out reading groups or math support with a specialist. The third tier is intensive, individualized intervention, which may include evaluation for special education services. Ask where your child falls in this system and what is available.

Request a follow-up plan. At the end of the meeting, summarize what was agreed upon. The teacher will observe for specific behaviors. You will continue documenting at home. You will meet again in four weeks to compare notes. The follow-up plan creates accountability and ensures the conversation leads to action, not just talk.

If You Are Not Satisfied With the Response

Sometimes a teacher will dismiss your concerns. The child is fine. They will grow out of it. Give them time. If your instinct tells you the dismissal is premature, do not accept it. Request a meeting with the school's intervention team or child study team. You have the right to request a formal evaluation for special education services at any time. The request must be in writing, and the school must respond within a legally defined timeline. You do not need the teacher's permission to make this request.

You can also seek a private evaluation from a psychologist, speech-language pathologist, or occupational therapist. A private evaluation can provide a diagnosis and specific recommendations that the school may not have the resources to generate. The evaluation is your property. You can share it with the school or not, as you choose. Having external data often strengthens your position in conversations with the school.

The Bottom Line

Raising concerns about a possible learning difference is not an accusation against the teacher. It is an act of advocacy for your child. Document what you are seeing at home. Request a private meeting. Present your observations calmly and ask questions rather than making conclusions. Request a follow-up plan. If the teacher dismisses your concerns and your instinct persists, escalate to the school's intervention team or seek a private evaluation. The goal is not to label your child. It is to understand how your child learns so they can receive the support they need.
